h1Title: Achilles Tendinitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Achilles tendinitis is the symptomatic inflammatory and degenerative disorder of the Achilles tendon — the thick fibrous cord linking the calf muscles to the heel and the largest tendon in the human body. Modern terminology favors Achilles tendinopathy because chronic histology shows degeneration rather than classical inflammation. The condition affects roughly 6-9% of recreational runners annually and up to 50% of elite middle-distance and long-distance runners over a career, alongside non-athletes who develop it from sudden activity changes, age-related degeneration, fluoroquinolone use, or systemic disease. Two distinct sub-types dominate: mid-portion tendinopathy (2-6 cm above the calcaneal insertion) and insertional tendinopathy (at the calcaneal attachment). Patients present with morning stiffness, pain during exercise that initially improves with warm-up, and tenderness with palpable thickening of the tendon. Eccentric calf-muscle exercise programs heal 60-90% of mid-portion cases within 12 weeks; refractory cases need shock-wave therapy, injections, or surgery. Untreated tendinopathy increases the risk of tendon rupture.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence 6-9% in recreational runners; ~5-7 per 1,000 person-years in the general adult population (Dutch and Danish cohort data)
keyStats.demographics: Male-to-female ratio approximately 1.4-1.8:1; peak age 30-50; affects 50% of elite runners over career
keyStats.avgAge: Mid-portion tendinopathy peaks ages 30-50; insertional tendinopathy more common over age 50
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 4-5 million new cases per year globally (extrapolation from cohort incidence)

definition: Achilles tendinitis (ICD-10: M76.6) — more accurately Achilles tendinopathy — is a clinical syndrome of pain, swelling, and impaired function of the Achilles tendon. Histopathologically, the chronic tendon shows degenerative changes (collagen disorganization, increased ground substance, neovascularization) rather than classical inflammatory cells, hence the modern preference for 'tendinopathy.' Two anatomical patterns are recognized: mid-portion (non-insertional) tendinopathy involves the watershed area 2-6 cm proximal to the calcaneal insertion where the tendon has poorest blood supply; insertional tendinopathy involves the tendon attachment to the calcaneus and often coexists with Haglund's deformity (posterior superior calcaneal prominence) and retrocalcaneal bursitis. Paratenonitis describes inflammation of the paratenon surrounding the tendon, sometimes coexisting with tendinosis. The pathogenesis is mechanical overload exceeding the tendon's ability to remodel, with secondary changes in cellularity, matrix composition, and tendon vascularity. Genetic predisposition (variants in COL5A1, MMP3), metabolic factors (obesity, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, hypertension), and medication exposures (fluoroquinolone antibiotics, statins, glucocorticoids) increase susceptibility. The condition is managed primarily by sports medicine physicians, physical therapists, podiatrists, and orthopedic surgeons, with input from rheumatology in inflammatory cases.

typesClassification.description: Most common form, affecting the tendon 2-6 cm above the calcaneal insertion in a relatively avascular watershed area. Presents with pain, swelling, and palpable thickening in the mid-tendon. Responds well to eccentric loading programs in 60-90% of cases.

Pain at the tendon insertion on the calcaneus, often with Haglund's deformity (bony prominence) and retrocalcaneal bursitis. More common in older patients and those wearing rigid shoes. Responds less well to eccentric exercise; heel lifts and shock-wave therapy often needed.

Inflammation of the paratenon (the synovium-like sheath surrounding the tendon) often presenting with localized swelling and crepitus on movement. Can coexist with tendinosis. Often acute and responsive to anti-inflammatory measures and rest.
Symptom duration under 6 weeks. Often associated with sudden activity change. Histology may still show inflammatory features. Best prognosis with prompt load management and rehabilitation.

Symptom duration 3 months or more. Histology shows degenerative changes — collagen disorganization, neovascularization, and increased glycosaminoglycans. Eccentric loading, shock-wave therapy, and biologic injections target the degenerative process.

primarySymptoms: Localized pain along the Achilles tendon, most pronounced 2-6 cm above the heel (mid-portion) or directly at the heel attachment (insertional).
Morning stiffness and pain on the first few steps after rising, gradually easing as the tendon warms up.
Pain at the start of exercise that initially improves with warm-up, then returns with prolonged activity or after cool-down.
Palpable thickening, tenderness, and sometimes a fusiform swelling of the tendon — visible nodule in long-standing cases.
Crepitus or a grating sensation along the tendon during ankle movement in paratenonitis.
Reduced push-off power during walking, running, jumping, or climbing stairs.
Worsening pain during hill running, sprinting, or jumping activities; relative comfort during low-impact activity such as swimming or cycling.
Tenderness with squeeze test (transverse compression of the tendon) — increased pain confirms tendinopathy location.
Pain on calf raises, especially single-leg, which is reduced range of motion or unable to complete in advanced disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Brief stiffness in the tendon area first thing in the morning that resolves within 10-20 minutes
Tenderness when squeezing the tendon between thumb and index finger 4-5 cm above the heel
Reduced calf strength: difficulty performing single-leg heel raises to full height
Mild thickening of the tendon noticeable to the patient or detected on examination
Increased pain after recent change in training intensity, mileage, footwear, or terrain
emergencySigns: Sudden 'pop' or snap in the back of the leg with immediate sharp pain — suspect Achilles tendon rupture; positive Thompson test (squeezing calf does not plantarflex foot)
Inability to bear weight on the affected leg or push off the toes
Acute severe swelling, bruising, and palpable gap in the tendon
Sudden onset of bilateral Achilles pain or rupture during fluoroquinolone antibiotic use — drug-induced injury
Severe pain with fever, redness, and warmth suggesting infection or septic bursitis

causes.description: Sudden increases in running mileage, intensity, hill work, or speed work overwhelm the tendon's adaptive remodeling capacity. The 'too much, too soon, too often' principle accounts for most athletic cases. Roughly 60-80% of recreational runners with Achilles tendinopathy report a recent 10% or greater weekly training-volume increase before symptom onset.

Tendon collagen turnover decreases markedly after age 30, with reduced cross-linking, decreased water content, and progressive disorganization of fibers. By age 60-70, tendon strength has dropped 25-30%. Insertional disease and spontaneous tendinopathy in non-athletes reflect age-related degenerative change.

Ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, and other fluoroquinolones cause tendon disorder and rupture in 0.1-0.4% of recipients, with a 4-fold increased risk and onset typically within 30 days. Mechanism involves matrix metalloproteinase activation and disruption of collagen synthesis. Concurrent corticosteroids and renal impairment amplify risk. Statins and glucocorticoids also increase tendinopathy risk modestly.

Foot over-pronation, equinus contracture, leg-length discrepancy, weakness of the gastrocnemius-soleus complex, and Haglund's deformity all concentrate stress on the Achilles tendon. Rigid heel counters in shoes and worn-out footwear can precipitate insertional disease.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus, obesity, hypercholesterolemia, hypertension, hyperuricemia, and inflammatory conditions (rheumatoid arthritis, spondyloarthropathies) all increase tendinopathy risk by altering tendon composition or vascularity. Diabetes raises Achilles tendinopathy incidence by 3-4 fold.

Variants in collagen genes (COL5A1, COL27A1), tenascin-C (TNC), and matrix metalloproteinase genes (MMP3) are associated with increased tendinopathy and tendon rupture risk. Family history of tendinopathy or rupture roughly doubles personal risk.

riskFactors.description: Recreational and competitive runners have 30-fold higher Achilles tendinopathy incidence than the general population. Middle- and long-distance running, especially with hill or speed work, concentrates load on the tendon.

Mid-portion tendinopathy peaks in middle-aged active adults whose tendon collagen turnover is declining. Insertional disease is more common in older adults with cumulative microtrauma and footwear-related stress.
Men have approximately 1.4-1.8 times higher incidence than women, reflecting both biomechanical (greater muscle force) and participation factors in athletic cohorts.
Each 5-unit BMI rise above 25 is associated with a 30-40% increase in Achilles tendinopathy risk. Mechanisms involve increased mechanical load and metabolic effects on tendon matrix.
Diabetes triples Achilles tendinopathy risk via advanced glycation end-product cross-linking of collagen, microvascular impairment, and reduced healing capacity.
Both elevated lipids and statin therapy associate with tendinopathy in observational data. Mechanism may involve lipid deposition within tendon and statin-related effects on collagen synthesis.

Ciprofloxacin and levofloxacin cause tendinopathy or rupture in 0.1-0.4% of recipients (4-fold risk), particularly with concurrent corticosteroids and in older adults. FDA boxed warning issued in 2008.

Seronegative spondyloarthropathies (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis) classically involve enthesitis at the Achilles insertion. Rheumatoid arthritis can also affect the tendon.
Over-pronation, leg-length discrepancy, worn-out running shoes, and rigid heel counters all increase tendon load. Custom orthotics and shoe modifications correct many of these.

affectedDemographics: Recreational runners: 6-9% annual incidence; up to 50% lifetime in elite distance runners (cohort data).
Middle-aged athletes (30-50 years): peak demographic for mid-portion tendinopathy.
Older adults (50+) with insertional disease: less athletic but with Haglund's deformity and rigid footwear contributors.
Patients on fluoroquinolones: incidence 0.1-0.4% with elevated risk in elders and those on concurrent steroids.
People with type 2 diabetes: 3-4 fold higher tendinopathy rate with often atypical presentations.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is primarily clinical. The patient describes typical pain pattern — morning stiffness, exercise-related pain, often a recent training change or systemic risk factor. Examination shows localized tenderness, palpable thickening, and pain with the Royal London Hospital test (pain on tendon palpation with foot dorsiflexed, no pain with foot plantarflexed) or with the squeeze test. The painful arc test distinguishes mid-portion from insertional disease — pain moves with the ankle in tendinopathy but stays fixed in paratenonitis. The Victorian Institute of Sport Assessment-Achilles (VISA-A) questionnaire (0-100 scale) quantifies symptom severity and tracks response. Imaging supports the clinical diagnosis when symptoms are atypical, severe, or refractory: musculoskeletal ultrasound shows tendon thickening (mid-portion diameter above 6-7 mm), hypoechoic areas, neovascularization on Doppler, and intratendinous calcifications in insertional disease; MRI quantifies degenerative changes and excludes partial tear. Plain radiographs detect Haglund's deformity, calcaneal spurs, and calcifications in insertional disease. The differential diagnosis includes posterior ankle impingement, partial or complete Achilles rupture, retrocalcaneal bursitis, plantaris tendon disorders, peroneal tendon injury, calcaneal stress fracture, and systemic conditions (spondyloarthropathy with enthesitis, gout). HbA1c, lipid profile, uric acid, and inflammatory markers screen for systemic contributors when symptoms are bilateral, insidious in a non-athlete, or atypical. The Thompson calf-squeeze test rules out complete tendon rupture — failure of plantarflexion when the calf is squeezed indicates rupture and warrants urgent referral.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination with palpation, squeeze test, and pain provocation maneuvers
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies tender area, swelling, and reproduces pain in a specific location to differentiate mid-portion from insertional tendinopathy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 5-10 minute outpatient assessment. Clinician palpates the tendon, tests range of motion, performs single-leg heel raises, and applies the Royal London Hospital and squeeze tests.

diagnosticTests.test: Victorian Institute of Sport Assessment-Achilles (VISA-A) questionnaire
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies symptom severity and tracks treatment response; score under 80 indicates symptomatic tendinopathy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 8-question paper or electronic form completed in 5 minutes. Used at baseline and repeated every 4-12 weeks during treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes tendon thickening, hypoechoic degeneration, neovascularization, partial tear, and calcifications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 20-minute outpatient scan with the patient prone. Painless. Real-time imaging allows dynamic assessment during ankle movement. Results immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for grading tendinosis, partial tears, paratenonitis, and excluding other posterior heel conditions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes lying still in the scanner. Painless. Gadolinium contrast occasionally used. Results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects Haglund's deformity, calcaneal spur, intratendinous calcifications, stress fracture, and bony abnormalities
Bedside test to exclude complete Achilles rupture
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: With patient prone or kneeling, clinician squeezes the mid-calf. Normal tendons produce passive plantarflexion of the foot; rupture results in no movement.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (HbA1c, lipids, uric acid, CRP, HLA-B27 if indicated)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for systemic contributors when tendinopathy is bilateral, insidious, or atypical
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw. Results within 1-3 days. HLA-B27 typing if spondyloarthropathy is suspected.

treatmentOverview: Initial management is non-operative in 90% of cases and follows a staged rehabilitation framework anchored by progressive eccentric and heavy slow resistance loading. Stage 1 (weeks 0-2) emphasizes load management — reducing aggravating activities while maintaining cross-training (cycling, swimming, deep-water running), short-term NSAIDs (5-7 days for paratenonitis or acute flares but not chronic tendinopathy), ice, and gentle range of motion. Stage 2 (weeks 2-12) introduces structured eccentric calf-raise programs: Alfredson protocol (180 eccentric repetitions daily — 3 sets of 15 reps with knee straight and 3 sets of 15 reps with knee bent, performed twice daily over a step) heals 60-90% of mid-portion cases. Heavy slow resistance training is an effective alternative (3 sets, 6-15 reps, 3 times per week with progressively increased load) and produces equivalent outcomes. Insertional tendinopathy responds less well to full eccentric programs because deep dorsiflexion irritates the insertion; modified programs that avoid dorsiflexion below neutral plus a 1-2 cm heel lift produce 60-70% response. Stage 3 (weeks 12-24) introduces sport-specific reloading and plyometrics. For patients failing 12 weeks of structured rehabilitation, extracorporeal shock wave therapy (3-5 weekly sessions; 60-80% response in mid-portion and insertional disease) is the next step. Injections include high-volume image-guided injection of saline outside the tendon (effective for stripping neovasculature in mid-portion disease), polidocanol or hyperosmolar dextrose (sclerosing agents), and platelet-rich plasma (mixed evidence). Corticosteroid injection is avoided in main-tendon disease because it increases rupture risk; it is occasionally used for retrocalcaneal bursitis under ultrasound guidance. Refractory cases (under 10% of all patients) consider surgery: open or percutaneous tenotomy with debridement of degenerative tissue, paratenon stripping, or excision of Haglund's deformity. Outcomes of surgery for mid-portion tendinopathy: 75-90% good outcomes; insertional surgery: 65-85% good outcomes. Postoperative rehabilitation is 4-12 months. Concurrent management of modifiable risk factors (weight, glycemic control, lipids, statin and fluoroquinolone exposure, footwear and biomechanics) optimizes outcome.

medicalTreatments.name: Eccentric calf-loading program (Alfredson protocol)
medicalTreatments.description: 180 daily eccentric heel drops (3 sets of 15 with knee straight, 3 sets of 15 with knee bent, performed twice daily) over a step or platform. Continue for 12 weeks. Patients work into mild-moderate pain (4-6/10) without exceeding it. Progressive load addition as pain improves.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-90% of mid-portion tendinopathy patients heal within 12 weeks; less effective for insertional disease (40-60%) where modified protocols are used

medicalTreatments.name: Heavy slow resistance training
medicalTreatments.description: Progressive heavy resistance program at 70-90% of one-repetition maximum, 3 sets of 6-15 repetitions, three times weekly for 12 weeks. Comparable outcomes to eccentric loading with potentially better adherence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to eccentric loading in randomized trials; 60-90% improvement at 12 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Extracorporeal shock wave therapy (ESWT)
medicalTreatments.description: Acoustic pressure waves delivered to the tendon, 3-5 weekly sessions, 2,000-3,000 pulses per session. Used for tendinopathy refractory to 12 weeks of loading. Both radial and focused shock waves are effective.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-80% improvement in mid-portion and insertional tendinopathy at 12-24 weeks in randomized trials

medicalTreatments.name: High-volume image-guided injection (saline ± local anesthetic)
medicalTreatments.description: Ultrasound-guided injection of 30-50 mL saline (with or without local anesthetic) between the tendon and the deep paratenon. Mechanically disrupts neovascularization. Often combined with continued eccentric loading.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 70-80% pain reduction at 6-12 months in mid-portion tendinopathy refractory to loading

medicalTreatments.name: Topical glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) patch
medicalTreatments.description: Quarter of a 5 mg/24h glyceryl trinitrate patch placed daily over the tender area for 6 months. Releases nitric oxide which stimulates collagen synthesis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest improvement in pain and function in some RCTs (effect size 0.3-0.5); headache common side-effect

medicalTreatments.name: Platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection
medicalTreatments.description: Autologous platelet concentrate injected into the tendon under ultrasound guidance. Evidence is mixed: large recent trials show no clear benefit over saline injection in chronic mid-portion tendinopathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Inconsistent — some studies show modest benefit, the largest (Krogh et al. AJSM 2019) shows no advantage over placebo. Not first-line therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course oral NSAIDs (5-7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily or naproxen 500 mg twice daily for acute flares or paratenonitis. Not recommended for chronic tendinopathy management because tendinopathy is degenerative rather than inflammatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Useful for symptom control in acute flares and paratenonitis; minimal effect on healing of chronic tendinopathy

surgicalOptions.name: Open or percutaneous tendinoscopy with debridement
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of degenerative tendon tissue and stripping of the paratenon. Open or minimally invasive (ultrasound-guided percutaneous tenotomy/tendon scraping) approaches. Used in mid-portion tendinopathy refractory to 6-12 months of structured rehabilitation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 75-90% good-to-excellent outcomes in mid-portion disease at 1-2 years; recovery 4-6 months

surgicalOptions.name: Haglund's resection and insertion debridement
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the bony prominence (Haglund's deformity), retrocalcaneal bursectomy, and debridement of degenerative tendon insertion. Performed open or endoscopically for refractory insertional tendinopathy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 65-85% good outcomes; recovery 4-6 months; sometimes requires tendon reattachment with anchors

surgicalOptions.name: Tendon transfer (flexor hallucis longus, FHL)
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for chronic tendinopathy with significant tendon degeneration where debridement removes more than 50% of tendon substance. The FHL tendon is transferred to augment the Achilles. Considered in older patients with substantial functional impairment.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 70-85% functional improvement; complete return to high-impact sport limited; recovery 6-12 months

alternativeTreatments.name: Polidocanol or hyperosmolar dextrose sclerosing injections
alternativeTreatments.description: Image-guided injection of sclerosing agents targets neovascularization in mid-portion tendinopathy. Repeated sessions 3-6 weeks apart. Effective in 60-80% in published series but requires expertise.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and dry needling
alternativeTreatments.description: Limited evidence; small randomized trials suggest short-term pain relief in tendinopathy. May be used as an adjunct in patients preferring non-pharmacological care.

alternativeTreatments.name: Custom orthotics and heel lifts
alternativeTreatments.description: Heel lifts of 6-12 mm reduce tendon load by shortening it. Particularly useful in insertional tendinopathy and in patients with equinus contracture. Custom orthotics correct over-pronation that contributes to tendon stress.

whySeeSpecialist: See a sports medicine physician, podiatrist, or physical therapist if pain persists more than 6 weeks despite rest, if symptoms are bilateral or systemic, or if you cannot perform single-leg heel raises. Refer to an orthopedic surgeon when 6-12 months of structured rehabilitation, shock wave, or injection therapy have failed, when a partial tear of more than 50% is suspected, or when full rupture is documented.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a sports medicine physician, podiatrist, or orthopedic surgeon with experience in tendon disorders and access to musculoskeletal ultrasound. A physical therapist trained in progressive eccentric loading and heavy slow resistance is essential for the rehabilitation phase. Ask about VISA-A score tracking, return-to-sport criteria, and surgical experience for refractory cases.
hospitalCriteria: Sports medicine or musculoskeletal radiology with point-of-care ultrasound
Physical therapy department with tendon rehabilitation expertise
Access to extracorporeal shock wave therapy
Orthopedic surgery with tendon reconstruction capability
keyFacilities: Physical therapy with running gait analysis
preventionStrategies: Increase running mileage and intensity gradually — no more than 10% per week.
Include calf strengthening (heel raises, eccentric drops) twice weekly in any running or jumping athlete's training.
Use a structured warm-up of 10-15 minutes including dynamic calf and ankle mobility.
Replace running shoes every 500-800 km or when wear patterns become asymmetric or compressed.
Address biomechanical issues — over-pronation, leg-length discrepancy, equinus contracture — with custom orthotics and stretching.
Maintain healthy weight (BMI under 25), HbA1c under 7% in diabetics, and LDL-C within targets.
Limit fluoroquinolone antibiotic use to clinical necessity; avoid concurrent corticosteroids; warn patients to stop the antibiotic immediately if tendon pain develops.
lifestyleModifications: Continue cross-training (cycling, swimming, deep-water running) during the rehabilitation phase to maintain fitness.
Do prescribed eccentric or heavy slow resistance exercises daily or three times weekly as instructed.
Use a 6-12 mm heel lift in shoes during active treatment of insertional tendinopathy.
Avoid high-impact and downhill running until pain on single-leg heel raise has resolved.
Stretch the calf gently (gastrocnemius and soleus) twice daily, avoiding extreme dorsiflexion in insertional disease.
Keep a symptom and exercise diary; track VISA-A score every 4-6 weeks.
Wear appropriate well-cushioned footwear for daily activity; avoid going barefoot on hard surfaces in early recovery.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet with vegetables, whole grains, fish, olive oil, and nuts for anti-inflammatory effect
Adequate protein (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) supporting collagen synthesis
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, peppers, broccoli) which support tendon collagen formation
Omega-3 fatty acids from fatty fish (salmon, mackerel) or supplementation
avoid: Excess refined sugars and trans fats which promote systemic inflammation and worsen metabolic risk factors
Excessive alcohol intake, which impairs healing
High-purine intake in patients with hyperuricemia who experience tendon enthesitis flares
Smoking — accelerates tendon degeneration and impairs healing

exerciseGuidelines: Maintain cardiovascular fitness with low-impact activities (cycling, swimming, elliptical, deep-water running) during active rehabilitation. Eccentric and heavy slow resistance exercises form the core rehabilitation program. Return to running follows a graduated walk-run program once single-leg heel raises are pain-free; start with 1-2 minute run intervals progressing weekly. Avoid hill repeats, sprinting, and plyometric work until VISA-A score exceeds 80 and pain provocation tests are negative. Strength training of the entire lower limb and hip musculature reduces re-injury risk.
dailyManagement: Perform the prescribed eccentric or heavy slow resistance program at the same time each day for adherence.
Apply ice for 15-20 minutes after exercise if there is post-exercise soreness above baseline.
Wear appropriate footwear with heel lift if prescribed; replace running shoes regularly.
Limit aggravating activities (running, jumping, hill climbing) per stage of recovery.
Track morning stiffness and exercise-related pain in a symptom diary.
Attend physical therapy appointments and follow-up reviews at 4-6 week intervals.
Manage systemic risk factors (weight, glycemic control, lipids) actively.
prognosis: Most patients with Achilles tendinopathy respond to non-operative care. Approximately 60-90% of mid-portion tendinopathy patients achieve substantial improvement with 12 weeks of structured eccentric loading; 40-60% of insertional tendinopathy patients respond to modified loading and heel lifts. Shock wave therapy adds 60-80% response in refractory cases. Surgical outcomes for the 5-10% who require operation are good: 75-90% return to previous activity level in mid-portion disease and 65-85% in insertional disease. Recurrence is common, particularly with rapid return to running or untreated systemic risk factors. Untreated chronic tendinopathy increases the risk of Achilles tendon rupture (lifetime risk 5-15% in chronic tendinopathy versus 0.1% in healthy tendons). Predictors of better outcome include shorter symptom duration, mid-portion location, athletic patient with good adherence to rehabilitation, and adequate management of systemic risk factors.
recoveryTimeline: Pain typically reduces by 30-50% within 6-8 weeks of consistent eccentric loading. Functional recovery (single-leg heel raises, return to walking and gentle running) at 12-16 weeks. Return to previous athletic activity at 4-6 months. Post-surgical recovery: 4-6 months for tendon debridement, 6-12 months for tendon transfer. Tendon thickness on ultrasound takes 12-24 months to remodel.
complications: Achilles tendon rupture in 5-15% of patients with chronic tendinopathy versus 0.1% in healthy tendons; risk amplified by corticosteroid injection or fluoroquinolone exposure.
Chronic recurrent symptoms with progressive functional limitation in 10-30% of patients despite treatment.
Partial tendon tear with sudden pain spike and need for surgical evaluation.
Reduced athletic performance and quality of life with persistent symptoms.
Postoperative complications: wound healing problems, nerve injury (sural nerve), re-rupture, infection in 5-15% of surgical cases.
Iatrogenic injury from corticosteroid injection into tendon — increased rupture risk; rarely justified for main-tendon disease.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) — Achilles Tendinitis
supportResources.description: US patient education from the leading orthopedic professional society.

supportResources.name: British Association of Sport and Exercise Medicine (BASEM)
supportResources.description: UK sports medicine resources and clinician finder.

supportResources.name: Royal College of Podiatry (UK)
supportResources.description: UK podiatry profession with patient information on tendon disorders and foot biomechanics.

supportResources.name: Australian Sports Physiotherapy Association — Achilles Resources
supportResources.description: Patient-focused tendinopathy education from sports physiotherapy professionals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Plantar fasciitis causes pain on the bottom of the heel, particularly with first steps in the morning and prolonged standing, with tenderness at the medial calcaneal tubercle. Achilles tendinitis causes pain at the back of the heel or along the tendon above the heel. Both can coexist but the location, tenderness, and imaging features differ.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gout (Tophaceous or Acute Crystal-Induced)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute gout flares can involve the Achilles tendon insertion with rapid-onset severe pain, redness, and swelling. Serum urate above 6 mg/dL, tophi, and joint aspirate showing monosodium urate crystals confirm gout. Achilles tendinitis is chronic, mechanically driven, and lacks urate crystals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis / Spondyloarthritis Enthesitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Spondyloarthropathy enthesitis classically involves the Achilles insertion, often bilaterally, with morning stiffness lasting over 30 minutes, sausage digits, sacroiliitis, and psoriasis or family history. HLA-B27 is positive in 60-90%. Mechanical tendinopathy lacks these systemic features and HLA-B27.

confusedWithConditions.name: Achilles Tendon Rupture
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute rupture presents with a sudden 'pop' and inability to push off; the Thompson calf-squeeze test fails to plantarflex the foot, and a palpable gap may be felt 2-6 cm above the insertion. Chronic tendinitis is a gradual, exercise-related pain with intact tendon and normal Thompson test. Acute rupture is a surgical or orthopedic emergency.

relatedConditions.name: Plantar Fasciitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Most common coexisting foot-pain condition; both involve enthesopathy and share biomechanical risk factors.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Spondyloarthropathy that classically involves Achilles enthesitis; important differential in bilateral or atypical tendinopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Can involve the Achilles with nodule formation and chronic pain; managed by rheumatology.

relatedConditions.name: Gout
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute gout can involve Achilles insertion; urate-lowering therapy resolves recurrent attacks.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Triples tendinopathy risk; coexists in middle-aged adults with insertional disease.

faqs.question: What is Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Achilles tendinitis is pain, swelling, and impaired function of the Achilles tendon — the cord linking the calf muscles to the heel. Modern terminology favors 'tendinopathy' because chronic cases show degeneration rather than inflammation. Two patterns are common: mid-portion (2-6 cm above the heel) and insertional (at the heel attachment).

faqs.question: What causes Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Most cases result from training overload — a recent increase in running mileage, intensity, hill work, or speed. Other causes include age-related degeneration, biomechanical issues (over-pronation, equinus), obesity, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, fluoroquinolone antibiotics, and systemic inflammatory diseases.

faqs.question: How is Achilles tendinitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical with typical morning stiffness, exercise-related pain, and tenderness on tendon palpation. Imaging (ultrasound or MRI) confirms tendon thickening, degeneration, or partial tear and is used for refractory or severe cases. VISA-A score quantifies severity. The Thompson calf-squeeze test rules out complete tendon rupture.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Progressive eccentric calf-loading (Alfredson protocol: 180 daily heel drops) or heavy slow resistance training, performed for 12 weeks, heals 60-90% of mid-portion cases. Modified loading plus heel lifts is used for insertional disease. Shock wave therapy and high-volume injection help refractory cases. Surgery is reserved for under 10%.

faqs.question: How long does Achilles tendinitis take to heal?
faqs.answer: With consistent eccentric loading, pain reduces by 30-50% in 6-8 weeks and substantial functional recovery occurs at 12-16 weeks. Return to previous athletic activity is typical at 4-6 months. Chronic cases or those failing first-line care may need 6-12 months or surgery for full recovery.

faqs.question: Should you keep running with Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Continuing high-impact running through pain risks worsening degeneration and rupture. Reduce running volume and intensity, replace some sessions with cycling or swimming, and follow a structured eccentric loading program. Return to running only when single-leg heel raises are pain-free and VISA-A score exceeds 80.

faqs.question: Are NSAIDs effective for Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Short courses of NSAIDs (5-7 days) help acute paratenonitis and pain flares but have limited role in chronic tendinopathy because the underlying pathology is degenerative rather than inflammatory. NSAIDs do not heal the tendon; exercise-based rehabilitation does.

faqs.question: Can a cortisone injection treat Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Corticosteroid injection into the main Achilles tendon is generally avoided because it increases rupture risk by 2-3 fold. Corticosteroid may occasionally be used for documented retrocalcaneal bursitis under ultrasound guidance, but not for tendinopathy itself.

faqs.question: What is the Alfredson protocol?
faqs.answer: The Alfredson protocol is a structured eccentric calf-loading program for mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy: 3 sets of 15 heel drops with the knee straight and 3 sets of 15 with the knee bent, performed twice daily (180 reps total) over a step. Continued for 12 weeks. Heals 60-90% of mid-portion cases.

faqs.question: What is shock wave therapy?
faqs.answer: Extracorporeal shock wave therapy delivers acoustic pressure waves to the tendon, stimulating healing through unclear mechanisms (possibly neovascularization disruption and growth factor release). Typically 3-5 weekly sessions. Improves symptoms in 60-80% of tendinopathy cases refractory to 12 weeks of loading.

faqs.question: Can Achilles tendinitis cause tendon rupture?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic tendinopathy increases rupture risk 50-100 fold versus a healthy tendon. The risk is amplified by corticosteroid injection into the tendon, fluoroquinolone antibiotic use, and sudden return to high-impact activity without adequate rehabilitation.

faqs.question: What is insertional Achilles tendinopathy?
faqs.answer: Insertional Achilles tendinopathy is pain at the tendon attachment to the heel bone, often associated with Haglund's deformity (bony prominence) and retrocalcaneal bursitis. More common in older patients and those wearing rigid shoes. Responds less well to deep dorsiflexion eccentric exercises; heel lifts and shock wave therapy are often used.

faqs.question: Should you stretch with Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Gentle calf stretching can help mid-portion tendinopathy but should be avoided beyond neutral dorsiflexion in insertional disease, which can compress the tendon insertion against the calcaneus. Structured eccentric and heavy resistance loading is more effective than passive stretching.

faqs.question: Can fluoroquinolone antibiotics cause Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, and other fluoroquinolones cause tendinopathy or rupture in 0.1-0.4% of recipients, a 4-fold increased risk. Onset is typically within 30 days. Risk amplified by concurrent corticosteroids, older age, and renal impairment. Stop the antibiotic immediately if tendon pain develops.

faqs.question: Does Achilles tendinitis affect both feet?
faqs.answer: Mechanical tendinopathy is typically unilateral, but 20-30% of athletes develop bilateral disease over time. True bilateral simultaneous onset suggests systemic causes — spondyloarthropathy, gout, fluoroquinolone exposure, or diabetes. Bilateral disease warrants screening for inflammatory and metabolic contributors.

faqs.question: What shoes are best for Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Well-cushioned shoes with adequate heel-to-toe drop (8-12 mm during recovery) reduce tendon load. Avoid completely flat or zero-drop shoes during acute flares. A removable heel lift of 6-12 mm can be added for insertional disease. Replace running shoes regularly to maintain cushioning.

faqs.question: Can Achilles tendinitis come back?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence rates are 25-40% within 2 years, particularly with rapid return to running, inadequate rehabilitation, or untreated systemic risk factors (obesity, diabetes, lipids). Lifelong maintenance with calf-strengthening, gradual training progressions, and appropriate footwear reduces recurrence.

faqs.question: Is platelet-rich plasma (PRP) effective for Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Evidence is mixed. The largest recent randomized trials (de Vos 2010, Krogh 2019) show no clear benefit of PRP over saline injection in chronic mid-portion tendinopathy. PRP is not first-line therapy. Eccentric loading and shock wave therapy remain better-supported options.

faqs.question: When does Achilles tendinitis need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is considered for patients who have failed 6-12 months of structured rehabilitation (eccentric loading plus shock wave or injection) and have persistent significant symptoms. Procedures include open or percutaneous tendon debridement, paratenon stripping, Haglund's resection, or tendon transfer in advanced disease.

faqs.question: Can older adults get Achilles tendinitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Insertional Achilles tendinopathy is common in older adults due to age-related tendon degeneration and cumulative biomechanical stress. Treatment principles are similar (modified loading plus heel lifts) although outcomes may take longer because of slower tendon turnover after age 60.

faqs.question: What is the Thompson test?
faqs.answer: The Thompson calf-squeeze test is a bedside test to detect complete Achilles tendon rupture. With the patient prone or kneeling, the examiner squeezes the calf muscle. A normal intact tendon causes passive plantarflexion of the foot; rupture results in no foot movement, indicating the need for urgent orthopedic referral.

metaTitle: Achilles Tendinitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Achilles tendinitis: heel and calf pain, mid-portion vs insertional types. Eccentric loading, shock wave therapy, and when surgery is needed.
